Transcrição Completa: 86 How to Scale Aggressively 6 24 24
[00:00] "All right, first of all, great momentum everybody. You guys are doing fantastic and love to see the growth. Um, today I have what I consider a great weekly group call topic for us. It is how to start scaling up aggressively."
[00:18] "Okay, what I've noticed is there's really a few different ways to go about scaling. Okay, there's like this safe kind of slow and steady, you know, highly meticulous. You are focused on a majority of the wrong things that are actually keeping you much smaller and slowing everything down, but doing that you still go through the same cycle as the person who's going very aggressively in comparison."
[00:45] "Um, there's people who are kind of in between hyperaggressive and going like slow and steady. And you would just call this more of like a regular pace and same thing. They deal with the exact same cycles as the person who's going slow and steady and and smaller versus the person who's going hyperaggressive."
[01:08] "And you got the person who's going very very fast and just pushing it to the fucking limit and once again experiencing the exact same cycles as the other two groups."
[01:20] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Scaling Cycles Framework"
NÚMEROS: 3 tipos de abordagens de scaling
VISUAL: Diagrama mostrando três caminhos diferentes de crescimento: slow and steady (linha gradual), regular pace (linha moderada), e aggressive (linha íngreme), todos convergindo para os mesmos pontos de ciclo
[01:35] "Now I want to start by introducing what these cycles that I'm referring to are when it comes to scaling. Let me start by saying this. Okay, I have seen this again and again and again and again. It is it is the most common thing that I see from the perspective of first of all being in I don't even know how many people's businesses over the years to educating people and being a consultant or just helping people as a student."
[02:05] "It is in my own businesses the same fucking shit again and again and again and again and again. Okay, so usually this is how it looks. First of all, let's just be clear on what a cycle is. A cycle usually has a start point and it has an end point."
[02:25] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Business Cycle Structure"
NÚMEROS: 3 fases principais
VISUAL: Círculo dividido em três seções com setas indicando progressão: Starting Phase (fase inicial), Middle Phase (fase intermediária com testes), End Phase (fase de repetição e sistematização)
[02:40] "Okay, and throughout the process of the cycle, we've talked about cycle management before. There's different steps in this process. You know, obviously when something's getting off the ground, it has its own part of the process that we'd consider the starting phase."
[03:00] "It usually then has - Who the fuck drawing on my board? Huh? You little turkey boy. I'm going to delete that. All right, don't do that. You then have this next part of the process, which usually goes through the middle part of the phase."
[03:20] "This middle phase, it's a little different. You know, this is usually when you're trying to find something that has officially worked and how you could scale it up or how you could change it a little bit, how you could uh essentially systematize it."
[03:38] "And you got the end part of the cycle. It's usually when things, believe it or not, are going really well. And you know, you've officially gotten to the point where it's a repetitive process that can be that can be done again and again and again and again and again."
[04:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Breaking Through Ceilings"
NÚMEROS: Múltiplas tentativas (1-12+) antes do breakthrough
VISUAL: Gráfico mostrando linha horizontal representando "teto", com múltiplas tentativas falhadas abaixo (linhas pequenas que não atravessam), até uma linha que finalmente rompe o teto e sobe verticalmente
[04:15] "Now, to be clear, how it looks when you're scaling is usually like this. You're going through a process where you're trying to figure a few different things out and then eventually you find something that cracks it. Okay? You break the ceiling that you are previously stuck at."
[04:35] "Okay? This process can look like this where there might be a dozen different things you attempt before you finally find the thing that breaks out of the cycle. Or sometimes it's like you might only have one to two things and then you break out of the ceiling and you go up."
[04:55] "Okay, the goal of these quote unquote cycles and trying to figure things the fuck out for what's going to help you break out of these ceilings are very simple. That's their main intention is you're just constantly trying to figure things out that are going to push the business up to the next level."
[05:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Examples of Breakthrough Strategies"
NÚMEROS: Casos específicos mencionados
VISUAL: Lista com ícones de diferentes estratégias: novo funil, nova estratégia de ads, afiliados, conteúdo Twitter + YouTube, cada um com seta indicando crescimento
[05:30] "As an example, this might be like a new funnel strategy, a new ad strategy. Uh Ben over here is trying affiliates right now is his current little cycles to fucking find what's going to push him up to the next level of the ceiling."
[05:48] "You know, homeowner Nick found out that content on both Twitter and YouTube, that combination of cycles helped push him up through the ceiling to the next level."
[06:05] "And then what happens after you break out is very simple and important to understand. You go through the exact same fucking process again until you find the next thing that breaks out of the ceiling."
[06:20] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Repetitive Scaling Process"
NÚMEROS: Ciclo contínuo e infinito
VISUAL: Diagrama de escada ascendente, cada degrau representando: teste → breakthrough → novo teto → teste → breakthrough → novo teto, em progressão infinita para cima e direita
[06:35] "And then how this looks simply put is a very repetitive systematized process of figuring things the fuck out and then pushing up to the next levels."
[06:50] "Now to be fair, unfortunately what sometimes happens is while we have finally figured something out and then it breaks us out of the ceiling, we change it and then unfortunately it kind of takes us back down."
[07:08] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Contraction After Breakthrough"
NÚMEROS: Queda não necessariamente ao nível anterior
VISUAL: Gráfico mostrando crescimento rompendo teto, seguido de uma queda (contração) que não volta ao nível original, mas fica entre o teto anterior e o novo pico alcançado
[07:20] "It doesn't always take us down to the previous level, but you know there's typically some contractions that can occur. It doesn't always just look this simple where you know it's like stairs in some instances."
[07:38] "Once again, it's like, you know, you you've officially broken out of the ceiling, but then you go below technically where you've experienced and you get a little dip. Usually, this happens because what broke you out of the ceiling was changed, replaced, or edited. Okay?"

VISUAL: Linha do tempo mostrando: Chris fazendo DMs pessoalmente (alta performance) → delegação para setters → queda de performance → Chris retornando para corrigir

Chris said something along the lines of like he has a bunch of setters talking to people and it sounds like they're doing a fucking job."

[15:08] "And then once you hit that ceiling, no matter how hard you try to push the fucking out of that business with that specific thing that got it up to that ceiling, it just it just doesn't have legs to go further."

NÚMEROS: Impossível gastar mais ou crescer mais

[23:10] "You'll just hit this fucking wall where no matter what you attempt to do to push it aggressively further, it just it just won't move. That's when you know you're at the ceiling and you got to iterate to what's going to happen next."

[30:10] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "When To Systemize: At The Ceiling Only"
NÚMEROS: Timing crítico de sistematização
VISUAL: Timeline mostrando três fases: Breakthrough (não sistematizar) → Scaling (não sistematizar) → Ceiling Reached (AGORA sistematizar), com X vermelho nas duas primeiras fases
[30:25] "Yeah. Now, why ask yourself why would you do it then? Because you're doing it on like a final version and not on like a basic version. Well, yeah, actually, yeah. I mean, that's very fair. That wasn't the point I was attempting to make, but that is fair to comment on."
[30:48] "The uh the point I'm attempting to make is whatever's making it work is what's making it work. Okay? You don't want to introduce other variables into the equation that could fuck up that momentum and growth because that in itself could become what creates the next ceiling."
[31:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Common Mistake: Changing What Works"
NÚMEROS: Erro frequente e caro
VISUAL: Gráfico mostrando linha ascendente (breakthrough), momento de mudança/nova variável introduzida (marcado com X), seguido de queda ou estagnação prematura
[31:30] "I've seen that many times and it's very unfortunate where something's working exceptionally well. It then has like a component of it or a variable of it that is changed or or a new thing that's introduced and it's no longer the thing that was pushing it up aggressively."
[31:52] "It becomes a different thing and then that in itself once again can limit the potential of that exponential that exponential growth happening that scale occurring. Okay."
[32:10] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Why Systemize At The Ceiling"
NÚMEROS: Lógica de timing

You have to focus on new iterations for what's going to break out again."

[33:52] "You'd log into it. It was an AI that would essentially look at all the different adsets and and campaign budgets that you had and it would recommend like a scaling budget and you would just press approve and this thing would fucking juice your campaign budgets every day."
[34:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "AI Budget Scaling Tool Process"
NÚMEROS: Escalação diária agressiva
VISUAL: Interface de software mostrando: análise de adsets → AI recommendation → botão "Approve" → budget automaticamente aumentado, processo repetido diariamente
[34:30] "Like every day that was going well and you were getting the result that you were after, you would just go in there and just scale the budget aggressively and it would it would somehow quantify like with AI what was like a safe a very aggressive budget that you could scale it up with."
[34:52] "And literally that's all that mattered. Like we had a very specific process. It was give away a free book, drive people into a 12-day email sequence between the 10th and 12th day. We told them to go watch a two-hour webinar."
[35:15] "No, that wasn't it, Nick. We told them to go watch a two-hour webinar. We would also retarget the people who opted in for the book to go and and watch that said webinar."
[35:32] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Revenue Split & ROI Timeline"
NÚMEROS: 70% email / 30% retargeting, ROI 30-60-90-120 dias
VISUAL: Gráfico de pizza mostrando split de receita + timeline de ROI: Dia 30 = break even, Dia 60 = 2:1, Dia 90 = 3:1, Dia 120 = 4:1
[35:48] "And we would make, you know, literally 70% of the revenue from the email. We make the other 30% from the retargeting ads. [36:15] "We we knew our ROI math to the tea. We'd be break even in the first 30 days, 2:1 in the in the second 30 days, 3 to one in the third 30 days. So So the 90 60th 90th day, and between the 90th and 120 day, we'd be 4:1."

[36:50] "So we knew it was a game of fronting cash. How much cash could we consistently front? And once again, just repeat that process. Do nothing else."
[37:08] "We had the same team the entire time. Everybody had the same responsibilities the entire time. Nobody asked anybody to do anything different. Every single person was just pressing the fucking gas on whatever they were doing that was working."
[37:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "When Changes Were Introduced"

[38:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Scale Mode → Maintenance Mode"
NÚMEROS: Transição de fases no teto
VISUAL: Dois modos lado a lado: "Scale Mode" (pessoas-chave executando) → "Maintenance Mode" (pessoas treinadas mantendo + pessoas-chave iterando novamente)

The people who are a part of the process and the systems and the and the software and whatever variables are used in the breakout periods, they just have to be fucking pushed until they hit that ceiling."
[39:20] "Once that ceiling is hit, and only when that ceiling is hit, you switch from scale the fucking mode to maintenance mode to like just keep like just maintain it. Don't let it drop. We know it's not going to go higher. We just got to keep doing this and it'll keep producing what it's producing."
[39:45] "But the key people need to now free time and attention back on iteration cycles to figure out what's going to be the next breakout. That's the point. Does that make sense?"
[40:05] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Unfortunate Common Mistake"

Let like whether you do it slow and steady, whether you do it regular, you know, no matter how you do it, when you go through this iteration cycle and you finally start to experience your breakout, this is the most unfortunate thing that I see."
[40:48] "Where where somebody usually the business owner will say, 'Oh, I need to right here, right now, start to offboard my key people who are getting this breakout to occur. [41:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "What To Do During Breakthrough Instead"

[41:30] "Like, it's like, no, dude. You need to repeat exactly what is making the breakout occur. You need to just the entire time assess like, is something going to break? Okay. Do I need to add or amplify? You know, do I need to do I need to put more resources into this?"
[41:52] "Like, is there another person that I need to add into the equation that does the exact same fucking as the person who's maxed out, you know? And then, like I said, what you need to look out for is when it kind of starts to plateau."

NÚMEROS: Diferença sutil mas crucial

[53:15] "Okay. So look, my point being when it comes to to switching from being like slow and steady versus just like regular levels of scale, okay, there's a slight difference between regular and aggressive. And the and the main slight difference is very simple to understand. It's how hard you try to milk that scale."
[53:40] "Okay, regular scaling is a lot more comfortable in comparison to aggressive scaling. Aggressive scaling is honestly a game of how much cash or resources can you front into that thing. And just trust the fact that it's going to continue moon bouncing. Okay?"

[54:40] "We don't necessarily believe that it's the fucking holy grail of scaling that's going to take our company up to extreme levels of new revenue. You know, we might think that it's going to tack on, you know, an extra like 100 to 200k this month."

[56:05] "So, we had already flipped, you know, a few deals. We were already making great money. And right away, just given the amount of ROI that exists that I'm telling them, I'm like, 'Look, fucking 10K. Like, let's let's spend, you know, 20 or 30 or 40K this month.'"

[57:10] "And, you know, going into that third week, same exact logic. I was like, 'Well, um, things are going exceptionally well. Let's continue to press the gas. Like, fucking spending 30k this month. Like, let's let's push it up to 50 or even 100.'"
[57:32] "And they were like, 'Yeah, absolutely. Like, let's let's press the gas.' So, we ended up spending about $50,000 in that first 30 days. And they cash collected on a $50,000 offer, $2.5 million. Okay."

[58:05] "That was a week overweek decision to continue to reassess the decision of okay what are the parameters for scale here that's the main thing I want you to understand that differentiates regular from aggressive aggressive is just simply put it's a it's an aggressive set of parameters that you're thinking with rather than assessing things like you know every few weeks or month over month."
[58:35] "It's like all right let's Look, let's look at a week-over-week decision here for on a percentage basis, like how much more we're going to spend. Like, think about this, right? Like, if we determined we were going to spend $30,000 for the month and we upped that budget to $50,000 in a week-over-week decision, that is almost 100% jump in additional spend."
[59:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Cost Per X Scaling Strategy"

[59:15] "That's like a 75% additional jump in the budget that we were otherwise expecting to put. Okay? Obviously, it made sense from a cash on casheash perspective, but most people, as an example, will scale like 10 to 30%. You know, they're not going to scale too aggressively."
[59:38] "On the other hand, when you have the opportunities to do so, those parameters have to be adjusted. How many of you as advertisers, as an example, just limit yourself to thinking with 10 to 30% as your budget jumps when you're starting to scale?"
[59:58] "You know, me, I have an entire strategy. I call it the cost per X strategy where I think to myself, what's my cost per result currently? What's my cost per sale currently? How many more sales or how many more cost per X results do I want to generate here?"
[60:20] "I'm going to do the math for what it currently costs times however many more I want. Relaunch that campaign with that new daily budget based on that math. And fucking we're we're gassing this thing with nitrous. Okay."

[63:20] "It's it's just simply put, it's so important to understand that the benefits of aggressive scaling are tremendously more in the favor of the business in a for-profit company than it is to be like slow and steady or even just regular growth in comparison."

[64:40] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Weakest Link Problem"
NÚMEROS: Um membro lento = toda empresa lenta
VISUAL: Corrente com elos, um elo marcado em vermelho (fraco), quebrando toda a corrente, com texto "You are as fast as your slowest member"
[64:55] "Have you ever worked inside of an organization where there's a few people on the same page about pushing it up to the limit and there's some random person who'd rather do it slow and they hold everybody back?"
[65:15] "It's that common quote inside usually of team sports or the military. We are as strong as our weakest link that absolutely applies in business when it comes to aggressive scaling."
[65:32] "You are as strong and as fast as the slowest individual in terms of their thought process and what they are tolerable of especially if they're in some type of decision-making role or a part of the decision-making roles that are that are scaling things aggressively or keeping it slow and steady in comparison. Does that make sense?"
[66:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "E-commerce Example: Multiple Departments Aligned"
NÚMEROS: 4 departamentos precisam estar alinhados
VISUAL: Diagrama organizacional mostrando: Advertiser + Business Owner + Sales People (todos com seta verde "GO") mas COO/Operations (seta vermelha "STOP") = crescimento bloqueado
[66:15] "Let's say I have an e-commerce store. Let's use the example that the advertiser, the business owner, and maybe there's some sales people that take calls occasionally and generate some high ticket sales for the business. Or maybe there's like some B2B component of the e-commerce store where there's some salespeople."
[66:40] "Those three people are all on the same page. They they found the breakout, they recognize the breakout, and they're like, 'Let's milk the fucking out of this thing. Let's push this thing up.' Okay? And they start that process."
[67:00] "But the person who's operationally responsible for the business, let's say the COO, okay, they're responsible for a few things, excuse me, they're responsible for inventory and they're responsible for the warehousing and just the fulfillment division of the business. Okay?"
[67:22] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "COO Becomes The Ceiling"
NÚMEROS: Falta de inventory = teto imediato
VISUAL: Funil de vendas funcionando perfeitamente (topo), mas bloqueado na base por falta de inventory, representado por caixas vazias de estoque
[67:35] "So, they overlook some pretty key components in this hypothetical example. The advertiser, the business owner, the salespeople, they're all like go. But the COO is like, well, I mean, when it comes to our like cash flow projections, you know, I don't really want to front as much inventory as I think I need to to be able to play into this potential growth curve that we're on."
[68:05] "Can that business scale aggressively in that example? No. They're going to run out of inventory almost immediately. You know, if the advertiser, the sales people, the business owner all start pushing it up aggressively spending and there's no inventory to sell. I mean that creates its own problem in itself which would be a ceiling in that example."
[68:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Predicting Ceilings Is Easy"

VISUAL: Diagrama de processo de negócio com diferentes departamentos, cada um com capacidade numérica, destacando qual departamento será o próximo gargalo
[68:45] "No, can you see how obvious that ceiling would be? This is usually how easy it is to predict when a person or a specific variable, if not tweaked to be aggressive, becomes the next logical ceiling. You have to look at all of it."
[69:10] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Grant Cardone Email Campaign Story"
NÚMEROS: 500K list, tens of thousands of responses
VISUAL: Representação de email enviado de iPhone, lista de 500K pessoas, onda massiva de respostas chegando, caos organizacional resultante
[69:25] "I remember one time way back in the day when I was working at Grant Cardone's office. I sent an email to the entire email list. It was like a 500,000 person list I think at the time that I sent this specific email."
[69:45] "And I I made it look like it was sent from an iPhone and I made it look like it was sent from Grant to the person and it was a simple question like something along the lines like, 'Hey, are you still interested in Cardone University?'"
[70:08] "And this was comical. We had tens of thousands of people respond because it looked like it was sent from an iPhone. And these are like good leads. I mean, these are people that indicated like, 'Yeah, I'm still interested, you know, and they were non- buyers.'"
[70:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Aggressive Action Without Warning = Organizational Chaos"
NÚMEROS: Toda a empresa teve que parar tudo
VISUAL: Escritório em caos com todos os departamentos (accounting, real estate, customer support, marketing, executives) tendo que parar tudo para processar respostas manualmente
[70:45] "So, without telling anybody, okay, as the marketer, I chose in that example, I wanted to do something aggressive, okay? And I got the result and and like, oh man, it was very sad almost. It's like immediately upon a breakout occurring, I immediately hit a ceiling because I didn't tell anybody else."
[71:15] "I didn't think or forecast what else in the business could potentially break, who else I would also need to step up and be aggressive. You know what ended up happening? It was it was uh I got got reprimanded pretty bad for this one."
[71:35] "I had to unfortunately after looking at this inbox and realizing that like literally there were tens of thousands of responses from like hot leads um everybody in the business and I mean everybody from like the accounting team to the real estate guy to the fucking customer support people to the entire marketing team to even I think I think some of the executives had to do this too."
[72:05] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Manual Lead Distribution Process"
NÚMEROS: 25-30 salespeople, páginas de emails divididas
VISUAL: Processo manual caótico: múltiplas pessoas logando no mesmo inbox → dividindo páginas de emails → distribuindo manualmente para 25-30 vendedores
[72:20] "Everybody had to log into this inbox. We had to divide the you know how Gmail has pages of like how many unread emails are per page. You know we had to divide the quantity of pages between each one of these people that was assigned this task logging into this inbox to read through all the responses and and roundroin them manually to salespeople."
[72:50] "There were 25ish maybe 30 sales people at the time of doing this. So then all of a sudden the sales people have to abandon everything they were doing and this specifically put attention into all these hot leads that were randomly generated."
[73:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Lesson: Forecast & Prepare For Aggressive Actions"
NÚMEROS: Previsão = melhor performance
VISUAL: Comparação: "No Warning" (caos, perda de dinheiro, abandono de tarefas) vs "Prepared" (smooth execution, maximum revenue, organized response)
[73:30] "I first of all didn't recognize that that would occur. I I didn't I didn't actually forecast the fact that that would be uh probable to happen with tens of thousands of responses. I also didn't forecast obviously that I would create an organizational problem that every single person in the company would have to stop what they were doing and put their attention on these hot leads."
[74:00] "Because as an organization we care about hot leads. You know, we we give a fucking about closing those people and talking to them after they expressed interest to such a direct question. Um, however, you know, because I didn't voice the fact that I was doing something hyperaggressive, once again, every single person collectively at the same time had to just all of a sudden abandon what they were doing and jump on board with this."
[74:35] "It would have been better, smarter, and it would have worked even better than it did if I would have prepared everybody or at least the division of people within the organization to anticipate this potentially occurring."
[74:55] "That way we could have milked that action that much further and and made that much more money off of that specific action and not potentially lost money from everybody having to abandon what they had to do."
[75:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Maintain The Curve = Prepare All Departments"
NÚMEROS: Preparação = crescimento exponencial mantido
VISUAL: Curva de crescimento exponencial suave vs curva quebrada/interrompida, diferença sendo preparação organizacional adequada
[75:30] "So as a direct example, if you don't go make people that are a part of the process of maintaining the curve, maintaining that exponential growth, okay, you're not going to experience the exponential growth. You'll hit a ceiling almost immediately. Does that make sense?"
[75:52] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Call Funnel Scaling Math Example"
NÚMEROS: 4 salespeople, capacidade de calls, show rate impact
VISUAL: Cálculo matemático: 4 salespeople × calls per day × capacity = maximum calls before needing 5th person, com timeline de 3-4 semanas para onboarding
[76:05] "Like imagine as an advertiser, you have a call funnel. You just choose to fucking triple the quantity of calls that are coming in for the sales team. You don't tell the sales team. The sales team, let's say, only has four salespeople."
[76:25] "You could have just done the math on how many more calls four salespeople could have handled in addition to the amount you already generate them per day. Because you didn't do that math. And because you also chose to double the budget, you only had a 72-hour window for bookings to come in."
[76:48] "You didn't have a fifth or sixth or seventh day because your show rate would have suffered. So now you double the spend. You fill up those sales guys calendars. You have to extend out the quantity of days that are available for somebody to book in. Now your show rate starts to suffer as a result."
[77:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Onboarding Timeline vs Scaling Timeline"
NÚMEROS: 3-4 semanas para treinar vendedor
VISUAL: Timeline comparativa: Sales onboarding (3-4 weeks) precisa começar ANTES de scaling action, não depois, mostrado em linha do tempo paralela
[77:30] "And in reality it's a three to four week process to hire somebody, train them to the point where they're actually able to take calls as a salesperson. So instead of anticipating a potential growth curve that was going to get created and milked, growth curve stops immediately and hits a ceiling."
[77:55] "Because you didn't anticipate a 3 to four week cycle that needed to be completed with a new salesperson being onboarded or a set of new sales people being onboarded before you chose to scale something. You got is this making sense to you guys?"
[78:20] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Growth Curve Killers"
NÚMEROS: Como aggressive vira slow
VISUAL: Diagrama de causa e efeito: Aggressive intent → Lack of preparation → Immediate ceiling → Falls back to regular or slow growth
[78:35] "Growth curves. Exponential growth being aggressive can immediately go from aggressive to regular or even slow and steady just based on you not preparing or anticipating the potential issues or problems that maintain the curve. Okay?"
[79:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Today's Lesson Summary"
NÚMEROS: 4 pontos principais
VISUAL: Lista numerada com ícones: 1) Choose aggressive, 2) Make organization think aggressive, 3) Prepare/forecast what will break, 4) All methods have same cycles, only scale differs
[79:15] "This the lesson today is very simple. Choose to do things aggressively. Make the organization think aggressively. Prepare, forecast, and project what is going to fucking up the aggressiveness of the scale. Okay?"
[79:38] "Slow and steady, regular, and aggressive all have the exact same set of cycles that they go through. It's just the amount of scale that occurs is limited or expanded upon based on how you go into the scale."
[80:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Scale Amplitude Comparison"
NÚMEROS: Tiny vs Mediocre vs Maximum growth
VISUAL: Três curvas de crescimento sobrepostas: Slow (crescimento mínimo), Regular (crescimento moderado), Aggressive (crescimento máximo), todas com mesmos pontos de ciclo
[80:15] "If you go into the scale slow and steady, it's going to be a tiny little fucking growth. If you go into it regular, it's going to be mediocre or regular fucking growth. If you go into each breakout collectively thinking, we are going to fucking milk this fucking you know? We are going to push it to its absolute limit."
[80:40] "You you get a much higher and faster level of growth that you're able to maintain when you go into it thinking that. Does that make sense?"
[81:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Know Your Cycle Position"
NÚMEROS: 4 posições possíveis
VISUAL: Diagrama circular de ciclo com 4 pontos marcados: 1) Iteration, 2) Breakout, 3) Scale, 4) Plateau/Ceiling, com seta "You Are Here"
[81:15] "And then like I said, you got to recognize the cycles of that growth. Where are you at? Are you in the iteration cycle currently or are you in a breakout or are you in the middle of a scale cycle or are you at the ceiling? Are you at the plateau?"
[81:35] "You know, when is it appropriate based on where you're at now to do what with this information? Does that make sense? Where are you at in your cycle with this information? What are you going to do with it? Okay, cool."
[81:55] "Questions on this? Feel free to unmute yourself and ask away or you can drop it in the chat if you're, you know, running or something. I don't know. Anybody going once?"
[82:15] "How many like how would you gauge when you're going through too many iterations? You know, I have like basically that empirical data for the affiliates. So, obviously, you know, that's the main focus, but um do you like try to just stick to like a couple things or, you know, focus on during the testing and iteration cycle?"
[82:40] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "How Many Tests Is Too Many?"
NÚMEROS: Não há número estático
VISUAL: Balança pesando "Time & Attention Per Test" vs "Number of Tests", mostrando equilíbrio necessário para ver breakthrough
[82:55] "Simply put, it's important to, you know, put enough time and attention into each test that you're trying to see if it's going to work or not and be the breakout. That's the main thing is you don't want to test too many things where you're not actually able to push them to the point where they do or don't break out."
[83:20] "You know, you got you got to have enough time and attention and and to be fair, sometimes resources to be able to see the breakout, you know, to start the breakout. If you're just spreading yourself thin between too many tests and iterations, then I mean, to be fair, you're probably never going to figure out if something actually starts a breakout."
[83:48] "You know, you're probably never going to even see a breakout because you're not you're not giving whatever you're putting attention into, uh, you know, once again, the time, attention, and resources that it deserves to to start that process. Does that make sense?"
[84:10] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Affiliate Example: Already Proven Breakthrough"
NÚMEROS: Já funcionou para outros
VISUAL: Ícone de affiliate com checkmark verde, indicando "já provou ser breakthrough para outros, merece atenção significativa"

You know that. Okay. So, that deserves a good amount of attention to see once again if you can experience that."
[84:50] "And then as you start to see the result of that, if it starts to break you out above your current ceiling, which is represented in revenue in almost all instances, if you obviously start to see that one particular cycle is producing a lot more revenue than the other cycles, that's typically the indication that a breakout is occurring."
[85:18] "And that's that's when you typically start to abandon the other cycles. And once again, prioritize the main cycle that's creating the breakout."

[86:15] "You know they have like different paid channels that they're putting time and attention into they might have like affiliate relationships or JB relationships you know they they might even just have like word of mouth that's occurring you know like each each business generally has, I'd say, like maybe three to four different total categories of revenue production."

[88:10] "You know, you put a fuckload of time and attention into that at that point and and at least resources to be fair. Sometimes it's not it's not actually additional time and effort that's required. It's literally just money that has to flow into it. Um so anyway, does that make sense?"
[88:35] "Yeah, of course. The affiliates are interesting because it's such a compound game where I feel like it's like especially when growing affiliates from zero, it's really slow and then I feel like it's just like a pop off. Well, that's what the like That's what those people told me."

NÚMEROS: Lento no início → pop off súbito
VISUAL: Curva de crescimento composto: linha quase plana por período estendido, depois curva vertical dramática (hockey stick growth)
[89:10] "That's usually how that's usually how it feels to be fair with any kind of cycle that you're that you're participating in. You know, there might be like early signs of life, but it does usually just have like a moment where it pops above that ceiling line. That make sense?"
[89:30] "Yeah. You'll kind of know. It's also important to recognize when something's just undeserving of additional time, effort, and resources. You know what I mean? Where it's like, ah, yeah, this isn't really working that well. I should prioritize something else."
[89:52] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "YouTube vs Affiliates: Different Cycle Lengths"
NÚMEROS: YouTube = long-tail, Affiliates = variável
VISUAL: Comparação de timelines: YouTube (12-24+ meses para resultado), Affiliates (alguns imediatos, outros compound 6-12 meses)
[90:05] "So, now let's let's use the example of YouTube, Ben. Like you attempted to do YouTube, you didn't put nearly the amount of time, effort, and attention that's required on YouTube to actually generate a result with it. That is a known longtail cycle, okay? As a known thing that takes a lot of fucking time to actually see to fruition and get a result."
[90:35] "Versus, you know, affiliates. Like, you know, to be fair, I've seen affiliate relationships where, you know, it's almost immediate that you generate a result. How about you know what you're attempting to do is you're attempting to collect a lot of affiliates in order to generate the result that you're after. So same thing it's a longer tail cycle."

VISUAL: Timeline com diferentes estratégias marcadas: Short-term (ads, email, retargeting: 1-30 dias), Medium-term (affiliates, content: 3-6 meses), Long-term (SEO, YouTube: 12-24 meses)
[91:15] "You have to be able to go into each thing also recognizing like what is the predetermined cycle time for this to actually see a result. You know is it shortterm is it longer term like you know once again how much how how many resources are required you know that kind of stuff. Does that make sense?"

📊 RESUMO EXECUTIVO
NÚMEROS PRINCIPAIS:
Mind Valley: $5M/mês estagnado há 1.5 anos
Dan Lok: $0 → $300K/mês via JV deals
Dan Lok: $300K → $2.5M/mês em 8 meses (0 testes intermediários)
Cliente agressivo: $10K teste → $2.5M em 30 dias ($50K spend total)
ROI inicial: 20:1 na primeira semana
Split de receita Dan Lok: 70% email / 30% retargeting
Timeline ROI Dan Lok: Break even 30 dias, 2:1 em 60 dias, 3:1 em 90 dias, 4:1 em 120 dias
Lista Grant Cardone: 500K pessoas, dezenas de milhares de respostas
Time de vendas: 25-30 salespeople
Produto Dan Lok: $2,500
Produto cliente agressivo: $50,000
Scaling conservador: 10-30% aumentos
Scaling agressivo: 75-100%+ aumentos
Budget jumps: $10K → $30K → $50K em 3 semanas
Onboarding salespeople: 3-4 semanas
ESTRATÉGIAS E SISTEMAS:
Venus Fly Trap: Estratégia de content ads usada para Dan Lok
Cost Per X Strategy: Fórmula matemática de scaling (Cost Per Sale × Desired Quantity = New Budget)
AI Budget Scaling Tool: Software que analisava adsets e recomendava scaling agressivo diário
Free Book Funnel: Livro grátis → 12-day email sequence → 2-hour webinar (dias 10-12) → $2,500 product
JV Deals/Affiliates: Estratégia de breakthrough inicial para múltiplos casos
Mind Valley Strategy Combo: Novos programas + Ads + Email blast + JV deals + Organic content
Week-Over-Week Decision Making: Reavaliação agressiva semanal vs mensal/trimestral
Scale Mode vs Maintenance Mode: Duas fases distintas de operação pós-breakthrough
Retargeting Strategy: Pessoas que optaram pelo book → retargeting para webinar (30% da receita)
ESTRUTURA:
Ciclos Universais: Testing & Iteration → Breakthrough → Scaling → Ceiling → Repeat
Três Abordagens de Scaling: Slow & Steady, Regular, Aggressive (todos com mesmos ciclos, diferentes amplitudes)
Fases do Ciclo de Negócio: Starting Phase → Middle Phase (sistematização) → End Phase (repetição)
Analogia do Bebê: Breakthrough = recém-nascido (máxima atenção inicial) → auto-suficiência progressiva
Weakest Link Principle: Velocidade = membro mais lento da organização
What Got You Here Won't Get You There: Cada estratégia tem limite de teto
Breakthrough Recognition Framework: Identificar momento exato de abandono de testes para foco em scaling
Departmental Alignment: Advertiser + Sales + Operations + COO todos precisam estar alinhados
Plateau Types: Temporário (continua crescendo) vs Sustentado (verdadeiro teto)
DADOS FINANCEIROS:
Cash Fronting Game: Investimento inicial com payback de 4 meses para 4:1 ROI
Revenue Contraction Pattern: Mudanças prematuras causam quedas mesmo após breakthrough
2-3 Months At Ceiling: Tempo típico de reconhecimento de teto verdadeiro
Time To Find Limit: 2 meses (aggressive) vs 12 meses (slow) para descobrir capacidade real
Cost of Unprepared Scaling: Toda organização parando = perda de receita + oportunidade
Inventory Ceiling: Falta de estoque = teto imediato mesmo com demanda
Show Rate Impact: Extensão de booking window = queda de show rate = menor receita
DURAÇÃO: ~94 minutos FORMATO: Weekly Group Call / Masterclass sobre Scaling Agressivo
